note: Evals target Tomako-specific constraints that the production goal depends on: reading-state protocol, page brief, runtime path, ToolSpec/registry/i18n wiring, UI Gate, Asset Gate, Content Gate, Launch Gate, agent result-source-of-truth, async tolerance, high-risk handling, slug/route, SEO/sitemap, and proxy diagnosis. External pSEO playbooks are advisory only and must not override Tomako Gate files.

evals.prompt: Let's build a new tool page: a website SEO analyzer. The user pastes their site URL and gets an SEO report. Just implement it.
evals.expected_output: Should NOT immediately code a frontend-only widget. Should establish the reading-state table, read p0-page-brief.md first, and ask the smallest blocking questions (input source, output contract, runtime ability, risk boundary). Should classify runtime as online LLM task + Skills-OL because it requires URL crawling/analysis/generation, not deterministic browser logic. Should specify the runtime path (POST /llm/tasks -> cc-connect -> Skills-OL script -> POST /api/skill-results -> SSE -> render) and that final result renders only from GET /api/skill-results/{taskId}. Should still cover ToolSpec + registry + i18n + five generated bitmap assets + SEO-first structure.
assertions: Does not default to a frontend-only/mock implementation
Establishes reading-state table and uses p0-page-brief.md before implementing
Asks targeted requirement-discovery questions or records explicit assumptions
Classifies runtime as online LLM task + Skills-OL with a clear reason (URL crawl/analysis)
Describes the LLM-task + skill-result writeback path
States final result source is structured /api/skill-results only
Still requires ToolSpec + registry + i18n wiring and five generated bitmap assets

evals.prompt: Make a UTM link builder tool page.
evals.expected_output: Should classify this as legitimately frontend-only/deterministic (string assembly, no crawl/LLM), which is the exception case. Should still require the full Tomako module: spec.ts + container.tsx + widget + registry.ts registration + widget registry + i18n messages exported from both tools/index.ts. Should require the Asset Gate's generated bitmap assets under public/tools/utm-link-builder-*.png, page-level references to those files, and browser validation that they render. Should keep H1/metadata/intro/widget aligned and follow p0-ui-gates.md, p0-asset-gates.md, and p0-content-gates.md.
assertions: Classifies as frontend-only/deterministic correctly
Still requires spec + container + widget + registry + widget-registry wiring
Requires i18n messages exported from both locale tools/index.ts
Requires generated image assets, page references to them, and image rendering validation
Does not skip SEO-first structure or UI/Asset/Content Gates just because the tool is simple

evals.prompt: I finished the spec.ts, container.tsx and widget for the new tool and registered it. Ship it as published.
evals.expected_output: Should refuse to treat first implementation as done without checking the image gate: a serious public page needs five generated bitmap assets, including a Hero background, one workspace side visual, and three inline visuals, saved under public/tools/{slug}-*.png and rendered with next/image or the project image component, localized alt, and stable dimensions. The workspace side visual must sell the value of simple input producing polished results, not be a frontend-coded UI diagram. Should not count ToolGuideVisual, code panels, gradients, placeholder prompts, hidden images, or unused files as satisfying the image gate. Should run pnpm tools:check-images, and verify sitemap inclusion + metadata alignment + slug/route before flipping status to published. Should also confirm capability is real (not mock) before publishing.
assertions: Flags missing five generated bitmap assets as a blocker (or requires recorded exception)
Requires a workspace side visual that sells simple input -> polished results
Rejects ToolGuideVisual/code panel/placeholder/unused file as satisfying the image gate
Requires code references and browser/Playwright proof that generated images actually render
Runs or requires pnpm tools:check-images
Verifies sitemap inclusion and metadata/slug before publishing
Refuses to publish while core capability is unverified or mock/misleading

evals.prompt: Build a privacy policy generator tool. To keep it simple and fast, let's just fill a template in the browser from a few form fields and show the result.
evals.expected_output: Should reject browser-only template stitching for a high-risk (legal/privacy/compliance) generator. Should require online Agent/LLM + Skills-OL with context capture (product/company URL, regions, data categories, processors, etc.), structured output including drafts plus missing-info questions, risk flags, and a human review checklist, plus visible disclaimers (draft, not legal advice). Should not provide a frontend local-preview/template fallback in the production flow. Final result must come from structured skill-result.
assertions: Identifies this as high-risk (legal/privacy/compliance)
Rejects frontend-only template generation for the production flow
Requires online Agent/LLM + Skills-OL with context capture
Requires structured output with missing-info questions, risk flags, human review checklist
Requires visible disclaimers / draft positioning
Requires final result from structured /api/skill-results, not template fallback or raw llmOutput

evals.prompt: Our agent-backed tool sometimes shows nothing. The frontend renders the result, but if the skill result isn't there yet it falls back to showing the raw LLM task message, and a 404 on /api/skill-results makes it show an error immediately.
evals.expected_output: Should identify two violations of cross-tool invariants. First: agent-backed tools must render the final user-visible result only from structured GET /api/skill-results/{taskId} with the expected resultType/schema, never from raw llmOutput / turn messages / local template fallback. Second: a temporary /skill-results 404, AWAITING_INPUT, or transient SSE error is not final failure; the UI should keep bounded polling within a grace window. Should recommend categorized failure states (submit_failed, upstream_unavailable, task_failed, result_timeout, result_schema_invalid, agent_writeback_failed) and schema validation before render.
assertions: Flags rendering from raw llmOutput / fallback as wrong
Requires final result only from structured /api/skill-results with resultType/schema check
Explains AWAITING_INPUT / temporary 404 / transient SSE error are not final failure
Recommends bounded polling with a grace window
Recommends categorized/layered failure states instead of immediate error

evals.prompt: For the new tool's guide sections and hero, I'll just reuse ToolGuideVisual and copy the layout from product-name-generator since it already looks good.
evals.expected_output: Should state that p0-ui-gates.md and p0-asset-gates.md are the canonical visual standards, and that a previous container or ToolGuideVisual is not the standard when the Gates are more specific. Should require deliberate hero/workspace decisions per tool, section-prototype variety, no nested card stacks, no over-complex forms, no heavy black selected states, no excessive icons, image subject filling the asset, and mobile QA. Should require real generated bitmap assets that are actually referenced and visible in the page rather than ToolGuideVisual as the generated image. Should note product-name-generator is a frontend-only example, not a model for high-context tools.
assertions: Names p0-ui-gates.md and p0-asset-gates.md as canonical visual standards over local precedent
Requires a deliberate hero decision rather than a default template
Requires section-prototype variety and no nested-card clutter
Requires real generated bitmap assets that are referenced and visible, not ToolGuideVisual, for the image gate
Cautions that product-name-generator's frontend-only pattern is not the default model

evals.prompt: We want to scale to 60 tool pages fast, basically the same page with the keyword swapped for each use case.
evals.expected_output: Should reject pure keyword-swap / doorway pages. Should require each page to pass the standalone value test and have a distinct user job, input pattern, output, example, or decision path; shared sections allowed but the useful core must change. Should keep everything inside the Tomako ToolSpec model (spec + container + widget + registry + i18n), not new content/tools MDX and not per-tool route files. Should recommend launching in reviewable batches with manual sample inspection. May use external pSEO playbooks as advisory background only, while applying Tomako Gate files on top.
assertions: Rejects pure keyword-swap / thin doorway pages
Requires a distinct user job / unique core per page
Keeps pages in the ToolSpec module model (no new MDX, no per-tool routes)
Recommends reviewable batches with sample inspection
Treats generic playbooks as advisory, not as the Tomako implementation

evals.prompt: The tool page is built and looks great. Is it SEO-ready to launch?
evals.expected_output: Should run a technical SEO sweep before claiming readiness: verify the tool URL is actually included in src/app/sitemap.ts (a known project gap to check, not assume), canonical + hreflang correct, draft/review/mock pages are noindex and excluded from sitemap and public index, H1/title/description/intro/widget all describe the same job, content is crawlable HTML (not only in images/canvas/hidden states), OG/metadata match the page, mobile has no overflow. Should not claim SEO-ready on visual polish alone.
assertions: Verifies the tool URL is included in sitemap.ts rather than assuming
Checks canonical and hreflang correctness
Confirms draft/review/mock pages are noindex and out of sitemap/public index
Checks H1/title/description/intro/widget alignment
Checks crawlable HTML and mobile/overflow
Does not equate visual polish with SEO readiness

evals.prompt: We renamed the tool from app-name-generator to product-name-generator. Now the old URL 404s for users who saved it.
evals.expected_output: Should choose the slug from the URL users naturally type/share and preserve the old one via a slug alias or redirect (resolving in one hop), rather than leaving a 404 or republishing under a wrong status. Should test the exact shared detail route for /zh/tools/{slug} and /en/tools/{slug}, confirm generateStaticParams sees the slug, and note the alias in release risk notes. Should not create a per-tool route file to fix it.
assertions: Recommends a slug alias or redirect for the old URL
Picks the slug from the natural user-facing URL
Tests the actual shared detail route for both locales
Does not create a per-tool route file or misuse status to fix routing
Records the alias/redirect in release notes

evals.prompt: I'm testing locally and POST localhost:3000/api/llm/tasks returns 502. So the local backend isn't running, right?
evals.expected_output: Should not conclude the local backend is down. Should explain that local /api/... may proxy to production/another upstream via API_PROXY_TARGET / NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL and src/app/api/[...path]/route.ts. Should inspect proxy env and route code, check the Network tab request URL/status, and test the direct upstream, then distinguish layers: local proxy failed pre-upstream, production/API gateway 502, backend up but cc-connect/LLM downstream unavailable, or auth/test-mode assumptions wrong. Should state which layer before diagnosing.
assertions: Does not conclude local backend is down from a 502 alone
Explains local /api may proxy to production via API_PROXY_TARGET
Inspects proxy env, route file, and Network request URL/status
Distinguishes proxy vs gateway vs downstream vs auth layers
States the failing layer before concluding

evals.prompt: Audit our existing programmatic SEO tool pages for crawl errors and pages returning 404.
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this is primarily a technical SEO/crawl audit rather than the programmatic-seo strategy + tool-page build flow. May reuse p0-content-gates.md and p0-launch-checklist.md, but should cross-reference a dedicated SEO audit skill for full crawlability/indexation work and focus its own contribution on Tomako specifics: sitemap inclusion, draft/review noindex correctness, slug/alias 404s, and shared-route reachability.
assertions: Recognizes this as a technical SEO/crawl audit task
Cross-references a dedicated SEO audit skill rather than doing a full audit alone
Still applies Tomako specifics (sitemap, noindex, slug/alias, shared-route 404s)
Does not treat it as a new tool-page build request

evals.prompt: An Agent-backed tool creates the LLM task and the Skills-OL script says it posted the result, but /api/skill-results/{taskId} is still empty. The script default api url is http://47.239.95.168:80 and production redirects to https://tomako.ai. Build/lint/dry-run/schema all pass.
evals.expected_output: Should treat this as a live writeback failure, not as UI done or schema done. Should inspect the actual Skills-OL writeback URL, protocol, redirect history, HTTP status, response body, and whether POST/body survived the redirect. Should require canonical HTTPS writeback such as https://tomako.ai or the team-approved HTTPS API domain, and reject hardcoded http IP defaults in prompts, scripts, systemd env, or examples. Should require a real live QA loop: submit -> Agent/Skills-OL writeback -> fetch /api/skill-results/{taskId} -> frontend render. Should not rely on build/lint/dry-run/schema as production runtime proof.
assertions: Classifies the issue as live Skills-OL writeback failure
Checks protocol, redirect history, status, response body, and POST/body preservation
Requires canonical HTTPS writeback URL and rejects hardcoded http IP defaults
Requires submit -> writeback -> fetch -> render live QA
Does not treat build/lint/dry-run/schema as sufficient runtime proof

evals.prompt: A Twitter GIF downloader page explains the workflow in public sections, shows a right-side 1-2-3-4 process visual built with React/CSS, and never says why users should choose Tomako over other downloaders, manual conversion, browser plugins, or scripts.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a P0 content and asset failure, not a minor polish issue. The page must answer why choose Tomako, define what makes a good Twitter/X GIF downloader, and position Tomako against those standards using concrete user outcomes such as clearer link handling, output/download clarity, failure reasons, privacy/copyright boundary, fewer steps, and result usability. It should replace process/technical 'how it works' content with value, outcome, boundary, and doubt-removal sections. It must require actual imagegen-generated bitmap assets under public/tools, not React/CSS/SVG/ToolGuideVisual substitutes, and the visual must not repeat adjacent copy as a numbered list. It should allow productized assumptions tied to real UX, but reject unsupported claims like official partnership, fastest, 100% success, certifications, real customer data, or exact performance numbers.
assertions: Treats missing 'why choose us' as a P0 blocker
Requires a 'what makes a good X tool' standard and maps Tomako value to it
Rejects public process/technical 'how it works' as a substitute for product value
Requires imagegen-generated bitmap assets rather than React/CSS/SVG/ToolGuideVisual visuals
Rejects 1-2-3-4 process visuals that repeat nearby text
Allows productized value packaging while forbidding unsupported factual claims

evals.prompt: The tool page has imagegen assets, but the images are basically UI screenshots: a white card centered on a large decorative background, many small UI cards/icons/text labels, and no scene, object, person, before/after value, or visual metaphor. The subject looks small after being placed on the page.
evals.expected_output: Should reject this as failing the Asset Gate even though imagegen was used. The core image must be visual expression first, not UI drawing or an information diagram. It should require the subject to fill the image without an extra background board/card/canvas, keep text to 1-3 short labels, avoid dashboard/card/icon piles, and use scenified, object-based, people-based, before/after, or value-metaphor compositions. It should make clear that page layout provides the container; the generated image should not bring its own large background or nested frame.
assertions: Does not pass the asset just because imagegen was used
Rejects UI-screenshot/dense-dashboard/icon-pile images as core visuals
Requires the subject to fill the image without extra background/card/canvas layers
Requires visual expression with minimal text, not logic explained inside the image
Encourages scenified/object/people/before-after/value-metaphor compositions
States that page layout owns the container; the image should not carry a nested frame

evals.prompt: A generated tool page puts every section inside one centered max-width container, so the hero, workspace, result area, image/text sections, FAQ, and CTA all occupy a narrow middle column while large empty space remains on both sides. The form width looks okay, but everything else is unnecessarily constrained.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a P0 UI layout failure. The page should not use a global narrow max-width wrapper for all Tool content. Only elements that need it, such as the form, long text columns, document reading panes, or FAQ text, should have local width limits. The workspace shell, right-side visual, generated result area, image/text sections, visual bands, and CTA should use the available browser width with responsive margins. The fix should remove or bypass the shared narrow container for page sections, keep form width constrained, and verify desktop and mobile layouts.
assertions: Rejects a global narrow max-width wrapper around all sections
Allows local width limits only for form and readable text/content panes
Requires result area, visuals, image/text sections, and CTA to use available width
Distinguishes form width constraints from page-level constraints
Requires desktop and mobile verification after the layout change

evals.prompt: A tool page has an H1, a working widget, a short value section, four FAQ items, and a CTA. But it only targets the main keyword. It does not cover keyword variants, supported and unsupported inputs, failure troubleshooting, what to do with the result, or related tools in the same workflow.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as incomplete SEO content. A serious public Tool page must cover the search-demand map, not just the primary keyword: core task terms, synonym/platform/object variants, input/output terms, failure and troubleshooting queries, boundary/privacy/permission queries, result-usage queries, and related next-step tools. It should require support scope, non-support scope, failure recovery, result usage guidance, and workflow-based internal links as crawlable HTML sections or clear in-page content. FAQ alone is not enough and keyword stuffing is not acceptable.
assertions: Flags primary-keyword-only content as incomplete
Requires coverage of core, variant, input/output, failure, boundary, result-usage, and next-step search intents
Requires support scope and unsupported scope
Requires failure troubleshooting and recovery guidance
Requires result usage guidance and workflow-based related tool links
Does not accept FAQ-only coverage or keyword stuffing

evals.prompt: A tool page hero places the H1 and intro on the left, and a large right-side card with tool category, reading time, updated date, and keyword tags. The page does not clearly tell users what concrete results they can get or why those results matter.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as both a content and UI failure. A serious public Tool page needs a clear 'what you get' / user-value module with concrete result items and their value, such as files, reports, drafts, checklists, recommendations, download actions, source links, export options, or failure reasons depending on the tool. It should not use reading time, category, updated date, keyword tags, or generic metadata as a substitute for user value. The value module should not sit as a right-side companion to the hero title; it should appear near the workspace, below the hero/intro, before the result area, or as an independent section/band where users are deciding whether to use the tool.
assertions: Requires a clear 'what you get' / user-value module
Requires concrete result items and corresponding user value
Rejects reading time/category/updated date/keyword tags as substitutes for value
Rejects placing this value module as a hero-right card paired with the H1
Recommends placement near the workspace, below hero/intro, before results, or as an independent section

evals.prompt: A page section titled 'What a good downloader should have' says the main standards are that users know supported links before input, know what is happening while processing, and understand possible failure causes. It is laid out as a left 1-2-3-4 text list and a right dense paragraph explaining the same points.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a P0 content and UI issue. 'Good tool standards' must prioritize user decision criteria such as stability, speed, output quality, cost/free usage, ease of use, compatibility, and trust/safety. Process transparency, status visibility, and failure explanations are useful supporting points, but they should not be presented as the core standards unless the specific search intent demands them. The layout should not use a left numbered list plus right dense paragraph that repeats the same ideas; it should be rewritten as compact standard cards, a comparison table, a single-column section, or moved into FAQ/boundary content if it is mainly support/failure/privacy explanation.
assertions: Rejects process transparency as the primary 'good standard' when user outcome standards are missing
Requires standards like stability, speed, quality, cost/free, ease, compatibility, and trust/safety
Allows process transparency and failure explanations only as supporting standards
Rejects left numbered list plus right dense paragraph repetition
Suggests compact cards, comparison table, single-column section, or FAQ/boundary placement

evals.prompt: A new public SEO tool page has only one generated illustration inside the page body. There is no full-bleed hero background image, and some generated image labels are in Chinese.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as an Asset Gate blocker. Serious public Tool pages require at least five imagegen-generated bitmap assets by default: one full Hero background image, one workspace side visual, and three inline visuals tied to concrete page sections. The Hero image should fill the hero/first-screen area, support localized HTML H1/intro overlay, and itself communicate the tool's function and user value with low information density. The workspace side visual should make users want to try the form by showing simple input leading to polished results, with very little text. Inline visuals should complement specific copy and vary in composition. Any text inside generated images must be English-only short labels; localized SEO copy belongs in HTML, not inside the image.
assertions: Requires at least five imagegen-generated assets for serious public Tool pages
Requires one full Hero background image, not just an inline illustration
Requires one workspace side visual that encourages form use
Requires three inline visuals tied to concrete page sections
Requires the Hero image to communicate function and value while supporting HTML text overlay
Rejects non-English text inside generated images
Keeps localized SEO copy in HTML rather than in image pixels

evals.prompt: A tool workspace form has a title, a paragraph explaining supported links, a field label, placeholder, full CTA, and then a large helper card explaining browser conversion, privacy, unsupported/private/deleted content, and failure boundaries. The workspace header also has a small decorative keyword/tag button that is not an action.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a P0 UI failure. The form must pass a 3-second action test: users should understand the input, action, and result wait state without reading long explanations. The form should keep only the task title, concise labels, example placeholders, required validation, and the primary CTA. Exposed configuration fields should be limited to the core required or high-impact inputs, no more than five; optional or advanced fields should collapse into advanced settings, more options, or a step flow. Support scope, failure boundaries, privacy/copyright/platform limits, and conversion explanations should move to page modules, result guidance, FAQ, or a lightweight tooltip/popover when truly needed. Workspace headers should only contain real task actions or real state; decorative badges, chips, SEO labels, or non-action keyword buttons should be removed rather than treated as required UI.
assertions: Rejects paragraph-heavy form introductions and large helper cards inside the form
Requires a 3-second action test for form clarity
Keeps form content to title, concise label, placeholder/example, validation, and CTA
Limits exposed configuration fields to no more than five and collapses optional/advanced fields
Moves support/failure/privacy/copyright/platform explanations outside the form or into lightweight tooltip/popover
Rejects decorative non-action badges/chips/SEO labels in the workspace header
Does not solve SEO keyword coverage by showing keyword tags as controls

evals.prompt: A page review starts by changing the hero layout, card style, image placement, FAQ accordion, spacing, and colors. It does not first identify the user's search intent, what information the page must contain, what value modules are missing, what questions users need answered, or which SEO demand clusters are uncovered.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a process-level P0 failure. New pages, rewrites, SEO optimization, and full-page diagnosis must be content-first: establish the Copy Brief and information module list from user perspective and SEO growth perspective before UI. The review should first identify search intent, user input/output, what users get, user value, why Tomako, good-tool standards, support/non-support scope, failure recovery, result usage, related next steps, and missing FAQ/search-demand coverage. Only after that should it decide layout, visual hierarchy, images, components, and interaction patterns. UI changes must map back to a user question, product value, SEO intent, or doubt-removal goal.
assertions: Rejects UI-first page diagnosis when content and SEO information architecture are missing
Requires Copy Brief and information module list before layout/visual decisions
Requires search intent, input/output, user value, why Tomako, good-tool standards, support scope, failure recovery, result usage, and next-step coverage
Requires UI sections to map back to user questions, product value, SEO intent, or doubt removal
Does not treat prettier cards, spacing, hero layout, or FAQ styling as sufficient page optimization

evals.prompt: An agent-backed tool page was implemented locally with new frontend LLM task code and a new Skills-OL skill/script. Lint, build, dry-run, and schema checks pass, so the agent says it is done. But nobody deployed Skills-OL to cc-connect or deployed/restarted the production backend/frontend, so the user cannot test it on the real environment.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a P0 deployment-closure failure. For agent-backed tools, local code completion is not enough. If the goal is testable/reviewable/launchable, the agent must include remote deployment in the task closure: deploy or restart the changed frontend/backend/Skills-OL/cc-connect layers when deployment docs and permissions are available, then run live QA submit -> Agent/Skills-OL writeback -> fetch /api/skill-results/{taskId} -> render. If deployment cannot be performed, it must mark the work as blocked or not yet testable, state the missing permission/server/key/release window/owner, and provide exact next commands. It must not claim done, published, testable, or launch-ready based only on local build/dry-run/schema/mock.
assertions: Rejects local implementation as complete when remote runtime layers are not deployed
Requires deployment/restart of changed frontend, backend, Skills-OL, and cc-connect layers when needed
Requires post-deploy live QA through submit -> writeback -> fetch -> render
Requires blocker status with missing permission/server/key/owner and exact next commands if deployment cannot be done
Does not accept lint/build/dry-run/schema/mock as proof of production testability

evals.prompt: Build a new SEO tool page for an email template generator. DataForSEO credentials are available, so just query a big list of 80 possible keywords and then write the page.
evals.expected_output: Should reject querying a large undifferentiated keyword list before understanding the task. The agent should first summarize the tool name, target users, task goal, user inputs, expected results, target locale/market, and assumptions back into the conversation. Then it should generate a keyword candidate table with search intent, why users may search it, fit, risk, and first-round query decision. Only after that should it query DataForSEO for the high-confidence keywords justified by the case, staying within the CLI hard cap and avoiding paid API calls when the requirement is still vague. It should inspect search volume/CPC/paid competition/SERP Top 10 and decide whether expansion is needed. The final Keyword Evidence Brief must be shown in the conversation before writing copy and page modules.
assertions: Does not query a large undifferentiated keyword list upfront
Returns the requirement summary to the conversation first
Returns a keyword candidate table before DataForSEO calls
Chooses DataForSEO query count from the actual case and stays within the CLI hard cap
Uses DataForSEO results and SERP intent to decide whether to expand
Returns a final Keyword Evidence Brief before copywriting and page generation

evals.prompt: Improve the copy of an existing Tomako tool page so it better matches user search terms and has data support. There is already a docs/seo-keyword-briefs slug file, but my local machine has no DataForSEO credentials.
evals.expected_output: Should treat this as a programmatic-seo content task, not a pure UI task. The agent should read the existing page copy, ToolSpec/i18n where relevant, and the existing SEO keyword brief before editing. It should not rerun DataForSEO or create a new scaffold brief just because credentials are missing. It should return the requirement summary, keyword candidate/brief summary, and current page copy gaps: keyword gap, user value gap, doubt/FAQ coverage gap, and modules to delete or downgrade. Then it should revise H1/title/description/intro/H2/FAQ/modules based on the existing brief and user needs. If no usable brief exists and credentials are missing, it should state that live keyword evidence is unavailable and avoid treating scaffold output as final evidence.
assertions: Uses programmatic-seo for existing page copy calibration
Reads and reuses existing docs/seo-keyword-briefs before querying DataForSEO
Does not create or commit scaffold as final keyword evidence when credentials are missing
Returns keyword gap, user value gap, doubt coverage gap, and invalid module diagnosis
Updates copy from keyword evidence and user needs before UI changes
States live keyword evidence is unavailable when no usable brief and no credentials exist

evals.prompt: A Tool page Hero uses a full-bleed generated background, but the main product document stack sits behind the left H1 and intro. Some important labels and result details are partly hidden by the localized text. The agent suggests making the whole image simpler and smaller.
evals.expected_output: Should flag this as a Hero asset/layout issue. The correct fix is not simply shrinking or over-simplifying the image. The Hero background should keep communicating what users get from the product, but place the main visual subject on the right side and reserve a clean left-side safe area for localized HTML H1, intro, and CTA. Important objects, readable labels, result cards, arrows, and focal details must not sit under the text overlay. Optional left-side decoration can be used only as subtle texture or small atmospheric accents, positioned away from text and never competing with the H1. Desktop and mobile crops/object-position must be checked so the subject remains right-weighted and text remains legible.
assertions: Rejects shrinking or over-simplifying the whole Hero image as the primary fix
Requires the main Hero visual subject to be right-weighted
Requires a clean left-side safe area for localized H1, intro, and CTA
Rejects important labels, result cards, arrows, or focal details under text overlay
Allows left-side decoration only when subtle, non-overlapping, and non-focal
Requires desktop and mobile crop/object-position checks

evals.prompt: Create a new SEO content page about Product Hunt launch checklist. It is just an article, no interactive tool. Please write it and add it to Tomako.
evals.expected_output: Should route this as a Content Page, not a Tool Page. It should establish the reading-state table and read p0-page-brief.md, p0-content-gates.md, p0-content-page-brief.md, p0-content-page-structure-gates.md, p0-content-page-writing-gates.md, project-map.md, content-page-implementation.md, and p0-launch-checklist.md. It should return a content brief with reader, intent, page type, content promise, unique information gain, cluster role, internal links, and CTA. It should decide whether the page belongs in content/blog, content/template, or content/mcp, then create/update MDX with required frontmatter, zh/en slug rules, public image paths when used, and internal links without locale prefixes. It should not create a per-page route or ToolSpec widget.
assertions: Routes an article-only request to the Content Page path
Reads the content-page-specific Gate files
Returns reader, intent, page type, promise, unique information gain, cluster role, internal links, and CTA
Chooses the correct content/{kind}/{locale}/{slug}.mdx implementation path
Requires required MDX frontmatter and zh/en slug handling
Rejects creating a ToolSpec widget or per-page route for normal content pages

evals.prompt: Let's publish 200 blog posts by swapping the keyword in one template, each targeting a different startup marketing term. We can add links later.
evals.expected_output: Should reject this as thin/doorway content risk. It should require topic cluster planning before writing, with hub/cluster roles, one page per distinct search intent, unique information gain for each page, user value, internal links, and a product/tool/template CTA. It should recommend batching with sample review, DataForSEO or other evidence for prioritized clusters, and noindex or hold pages that do not pass the standalone value test. It should not proceed to mass-generate isolated MDX files.
assertions: Rejects keyword-swap template pages as thin/doorway risk
Requires topic cluster planning before generation
Requires distinct search intent and unique information gain per page
Requires internal links and Tomako CTA before publishing
Recommends batch launch and sample review
Does not mass-generate isolated MDX files

evals.prompt: Improve an existing Tomako blog post so the copy better matches search terms and user needs. We have no DataForSEO credentials on this machine, but there may be an existing keyword brief.
evals.expected_output: Should treat this as content-page copy calibration. It should read the existing MDX, p0-content-gates.md, p0-content-page-writing-gates.md, content-page-implementation.md, and any existing docs/seo-keyword-briefs/{slug}.md/.json before editing. It should not create a scaffold brief as final evidence when credentials are missing. It should return a gap diagnosis: keyword gap, user intent gap, information gain gap, trust/source gap, internal-link gap, and CTA gap. Then it should revise title, description, intro, H2s, body, FAQ, and internal links according to available evidence and clearly state live keyword data limitations.
assertions: Routes existing blog copy work to content-page copy calibration
Reads the current MDX and existing keyword brief before editing
Does not treat no-key scaffold output as final evidence
Returns keyword, intent, information gain, trust/source, internal-link, and CTA gaps
Updates frontmatter/body/FAQ/internal links from evidence and user needs
States limitations when live keyword data is unavailable
